articles.author: E. Eduardo Castillo, Elena Becatoros
articles.description: U.S. President Donald Trump is threatening Iran with more bombing if it doesn’t reopen strait after report that agreement is emerging to end war. Trump posted on social media Wednesday that the war with Iran could soon end and oil and natural gas shipments could restart. But that all depends on Iran accepting a reported agreement that the U.S. president did not detail. Trump wrote: “If they don’t agree, the bombing starts."
articles.title: Trump threatens Iran with bombing if it doesn’t reopen Strait of Hormuz

articles.author: Thomas Adamson
articles.description: The French armed forces say that France’s aircraft carrier strike group is moving south of the Suez Canal and into the Red Sea in preparation for a potential future mission as part of a French-British plan for the Strait of Hormuz. The southward repositioning of the nuclear-powered Charles de Gaulle and its escorts is the latest stage of a Middle East deployment first announced by President Emmanuel Macron in a televised address on March 3. That was the day before Iran closed the strait. What is new is the move south of Suez, putting France’s only carrier closer to the Persian Gulf chokepoint of Hormuz.
articles.title: French carrier group moves south of Suez ahead of a possible Hormuz mission

articles.author: Yuri Kageyama, Matt Ott
articles.description: Markets on Wall Street soared overnight and U.S. crude prices plunged 13% after China’s foreign minister called for a comprehensive ceasefire in the Iran war, the latest spark of optimism that the two-month war could end soon. Futures for the S&P 500 rose 1% before the opening bell Wednesday, while futures for the Dow climbed 1.2%. Nasdaq futures jumped 1.7%. Wang Yi, speaking after meeting with Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi, said China was “deeply distressed” by the conflict. U.S. crude fell more than $13 to under $90 a barrel. Brent crude, the international standard, lost $12.66 to $97.21 a barrel.
articles.title: Global shares gain and oil prices fall as investors pin hopes on progress in ending the Iran war

articles.author: Kanis Leung, Huizhong Wu
articles.description: China’s diplomatic role in the Iran war has come into sharper focus after talks between Chinese and Iranian foreign ministers on Wednesday, days before U.S. President Donald Trump is expected to meet his Chinese counterpart Xi Jinping. Though not an official mediator, China has played a key role in de-escalating tensions. The Trump administration is urging China to influence Iran to open the Strait of Hormuz. Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi on Wednesday called for a ceasefire, emphasizing the international community's concern over the strait's reopening. The timing of these talks is crucial, as Trump and Xi are set to meet next week.
articles.title: China steps up Iran war diplomacy ahead of Trump-Xi summit

articles.author: Eric Tucker, Jennifer Peltz
articles.description: The FBI served a search warrant at the office of the Virginia Senate leader's office as part of a corruption investigation. That's according to a person familiar with the matter who was not authorized to discuss an ongoing investigation by name and spoke to The Associated Press on condition of anonymity. The search Wednesday at Virginia Sen. L. Louise Lucas’s district office in Portsmouth comes after the Democrat helped lead the state's recent redistricting. The FBI said only that it was conducting a court-authorized search warrant in Portsmouth.
articles.title: AP Source: Office of Virginia Senate leader searched by FBI as part of corruption probe

articles.author: Bridget Brown, Michael Warren, Luena Rodriguez-Feo Vileira
articles.description: Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick is appearing before a House committee investigating Jeffrey Epstein as lawmakers seek answers for Lutnick's contact with him in the years after 2008.
articles.title: Live updates: Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick to appear before House panel over Epstein ties

articles.author: Linley Sanders, Tim Sullivan, Mike Catalini
articles.description: A new AP-NORC poll finds about 6 in 10 U.S. adults say the country is no longer a great place for immigrants, though they believe it used to be. Only about 3 in 10 U.S. adults say the U.S. is a great place for immigrants, while about 1 in 10 say it never was. The belief that America used to be great for immigrants is more prominent among Democrats and independents, as well as among people who were born outside of the U.S. The poll also finds about one-third of U.S. adults say that someone they know personally has started carrying proof of their immigration status, been detained or deported, changed travel plans, or significantly changed routines amid heightened immigration enforcement.
articles.title: Many know someone affected by Trump immigration push: new poll

articles.author: Dylan Jackson/the Atlanta Journal-constitution, Jason Dearen/ap, Justin Price/the Atlanta Journal-constitution
articles.description: State officials knew nearly two decades ago that toxic chemicals called PFAS were spreading from the carpet mills of northwest Georgia into rivers that are the region's main source of drinking water. Local residents did not know. Now the odorless, colorless chemicals that were in their tap water are in the blood of some of them at levels higher than health guidelines consider safe. An investigation by The Associated Press, The Atlanta Journal-Constitution and FRONTLINE (PBS) has found Georgia’s Environmental Protection Division did little to confront the pollution, even as concerns grew among scientists and federal regulators about the dangers of PFAS.
articles.title: Investigation finds Georgia knew carpet mill chemicals were polluting local water

Local residents did not know. Now the odorless, colorless chemicals that were in their tap water are in the blood of some of them at levels higher than health guidelines consider safe. An investigation by The Associated Press, The Atlanta Journal-Constitution and FRONTLINE (PBS) has found Georgia’s Environmental Protection Division did little to confront the pollution, even as concerns grew among scientists and federal regulators about the dangers of PFAS.
articles.title: Georgia didn't take action on PFAS water pollution from carpet mills, investigation shows

articles.description: The Georgia carpet industry’s long use of PFAS, known as forever chemicals, polluted drinking water and the environment across swaths of the South.
articles.title: The toxic legacy of Georgia’s carpet empire
